# 14th Century European Clothing & Armor Visual Reference

> Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1300%E2%80%931400_in_European_fashion
> Fetched: 2026-03-27

## Overview

The 14th century marks a pivotal transformation in European fashion history. Costume historian James Laver identified the mid-14th century as the moment when recognizable "fashion" first emerged in European clothing. The draped garments and straight seams of previous centuries gave way to curved seams and the beginnings of tailoring, allowing clothing to closely fit the human form. Lacing and buttons enabled increasingly snug fits. This document provides visual reference for game character artists working on medieval-themed projects.

---

## 1. Materials & Fabrics

### Primary Textiles

| Material | Usage | Notes |
|----------|-------|-------|
| **Wool** | Dominant textile for all classes | Excellent dyeability and insulation; available in many weaves |
| **Linen** | Undergarments (chemises, braies) | Cool, breathable; used next to skin |
| **Silk** | Luxury outer garments for nobility | Expensive import from Italy and the East; featured Ottoman-influenced roundel patterns |
| **Velvet** | Court garments and ceremonial wear | Often embroidered with gold thread |

### Fur Linings

Fur linings provided warmth and indicated social status:

- **Vair** (squirrel fur): Most popular throughout most of the century; grey and white pattern
- **Sable and Marten**: Darker furs that became fashionable for men around 1380
- **Ermine**: Reserved for royalty; white fur with distinctive black-tipped tails
- **Miniver**: White belly fur of squirrel; used for lining ceremonial robes

### Decorative Techniques

- **Woodblock printing** on cloth
- **Embroidery** in wool, silk, and precious metals (gold and silver thread)
- **Brocade**: Woven patterns with raised metallic designs
- **Dagging**: Decorative cut edges on garments, creating leaf-like or floral scalloped borders
- **Mi-parti / Parti-coloring**: Garments divided into contrasting colors on each side; also applied to hose with different colors on each leg

### Notable Example

The Duke of Burgundy was recorded wearing "a velvet doublet embroidered with forty sheep" and swans with pearl bells. Edward III's workshop at the Tower of London produced robes of red velvet "embroidered with clouds of silver and eagles of pearl and gold."

---

## 2. Men's Clothing

### Undergarments & Base Layers

- **Braies**: Loose linen breeches held at the waist by a belt or drawstring; the fundamental undergarment
- **Shirt (Chemise)**: Linen undergarment worn next to skin beneath all outer layers; typically white or undyed

### Leg Wear

- **Hose (Chausses)**: Wool leg coverings, often brightly colored
- Early in the century: separate stockings tied to braies or belt
- As hemlines shortened mid-century: evolved into joined hose resembling modern tights
- Often had integral leather soles functioning as shoes
- **Parti-colored hose**: Different color on each leg, a distinctive fashion statement

### Torso Garments

#### The Doublet (c. 1340 onward)

- Buttoned, hip-length jacket worn over the shirt and hose
- Fitted closely to the torso through tailoring
- Regional name variants: **pourpoint** (France), **cotehardie** (England), **jaqueta** (Spain), **jubon** (Spain)
- Padding in chest and shoulders for a masculine silhouette
- Sleeves fitted tightly, often buttoned from elbow to wrist

#### The Tunic / Kirtle

- Wool outer robe worn at varying lengths depending on class and period
- Early century: knee-length or longer
- Mid-century shift: dramatically shortened, becoming scandalously short
- A 1350 chronicle records: "men, in particular, noblemen and their squires, took to wearing tunics so short and tight that they revealed what modesty bids us hide"

#### The Houppelande (c. 1380 onward)

- Full-length or knee-length robe with voluminous fabric from the shoulders
- Very full trailing sleeves (bagpipe or funnel-shaped)
- High standing collar, often reaching the ears
- Belted at the waist
- Richly lined with fur
- Favored at the English court; critics condemned the extravagant sleeves

### Outer Garments

- **Cloaks and Mantles**: Worn over other garments for warmth and ceremony
- **Surcoat**: Worn over armor or as formal outer layer
- **Tabard**: Sleeveless overgarment displaying heraldic arms

### The Arming Coat (Jupon/Gipon)

Edward the Black Prince's surviving arming coat features:
- Quilted construction with wool stuffing
- Silk lining
- Appliqued embroidered arms on velvet fields
- Short, fitted garment originally worn under armor
- Became fashionable as outer wear circa 1360 until early 1400s

---

## 3. Women's Clothing

### Undergarments

- **Chemise / Smock**: Linen or wool inner layer worn next to skin; some fitted, some loose
- **Breast girdle / Breast band**: Possible precursor to the bra; mentioned in some sources
- **Hose / Stockings**: Reached only to the knee; held up by garters

### Main Garments

#### The Kirtle / Cotte

- Ankle or floor-length gown with long, fitted sleeves reaching to the knuckles
- Fitted versions used triangular fabric inserts called **gores** for hem width without waist bulk
- Formal occasions featured long trains
- Laced or buttoned up the front or back for a close fit

#### The Cotehardie (Fitted Overgown)

- Closely fitted outer garment worn over the kirtle
- Featured hanging sleeves that narrowed into decorative streamers called **tippets**
- Tippets gained floral or leaf-like dagged edges by century's end
- Often had decorative buttons down the front

#### The Sideless Surcoat (Tabard)

- Evolved from the 13th-century cyclas (unfitted rectangle with head opening)
- By early 14th century: sewn sides creating a sleeveless overgown
- Deep armholes revealed the fitted kirtle beneath
- Often lined with fur; a formal and ceremonial garment

#### The Houppelande (c. 1390 onward for women)

- Adopted from men's fashion late in the century
- Women invariably wore houppelandes floor-length
- Waistline rose to just under the bust (empire line)
- Very wide hanging sleeves (angel sleeves)
- High collar

### Outer Garments

- **Cloaks and Mantles**: Often fur-lined; worn for outdoor warmth
- Fastened at the throat with brooches or cords

---

## 4. Headwear

### Women's Headwear (Northern & Western Europe)

Married women were socially required to cover their hair:

| Item | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Barbet** | Linen band passing under the chin and pinned atop the head; descended from the wimple; fell from fashion by mid-century |
| **Fillet / Headband** | Stiffened linen band worn across the forehead, often paired with barbet |
| **Coif** | Close-fitting linen cap covering the hair |
| **Couvrechef / Kerchief** | Cloth veil worn over coif |
| **Wimple** | Cloth covering throat and chin; by 14th century worn primarily by older women, widows, and nuns |
| **Crespine / Caul** | Thick hairnet or snood evolving into jeweled mesh confining hair at sides and back of head |
| **Veil** | Draped fabric over the head; various lengths and opacities |

**Italian Exception**: Italian women were permitted to leave hair uncovered. They twisted long hair with cords or ribbons and wrapped twists around the head without caps or veils. Braided styles were common. Only older women and widows wore veils and wimples.

### Men's Headwear

| Item | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Chaperon** | Evolved from a utilitarian hood with small cape; transformed into a fashionable hat with the face opening repositioned atop the head; worn by wealthy townspeople |
| **Coif** | Simple linen cap; worn by all classes |
| **Hood** | Practical head covering attached to a short cape (liripipe) |
| **Liripipe** | The long tail of a hood; grew increasingly elongated as a fashion statement |

---

## 5. Accessories & Footwear

### Accessories

- **Belts**: Worn low at the hips; included hanging pouches/purses and long daggers positioned diagonally forward
- **Livery badges**: Personal emblems distributed by lords; Richard II's badge was the white hart (deer), worn as decorative jewelry by courtiers
- **Purses**: Leather or fabric pouches hung from the belt
- **Handkerchief**: Richard II is credited with inventing it, documented as "little pieces [of cloth] for the lord King to wipe and clean his nose"
- **Jewelry**: Brooches, rings, decorative belt buckles, jeweled buttons

### Footwear

- **Turnshoes**: Primary footwear; leather construction turned inside-out during making for smooth exterior
- **Pointed toes**: Came to long points through the century, often stuffed to maintain shape
- **Poulaines**: Extremely pointed shoes that would dominate 15th-century fashion
- **Pattens**: Carved wooden-soled clog-like overshoes worn outdoors over leather shoes (which were not waterproof)
- **Hose with soles**: Integrated leather soles on hose functioned as indoor shoes

---

## 6. Armor & Military Dress

### Armor Evolution in the 14th Century

The 14th century is characterized as the **"age of experimentation"** in European armor development. The century saw a gradual transition from mail-dominant armor to increasing use of plate.

### Armor Types & Components

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Mail Hauberk** | Full shirt of interlocking metal rings; the foundational armor layer throughout the century |
| **Cuir Bouilli** | Hardened (boiled) leather formed into protective shapes; used for limb defenses |
| **Quilted Fabric** | Padded garments (gambesons/aketon) worn under mail for cushioning |
| **Plate Armor** | Individual metal plates protecting specific body parts; usage increased throughout the century |
| **Coat of Plates** | Multiple metal plates riveted inside a fabric or leather garment |

### Armor Pieces by Body Area

**Head:**
- **Great Helm**: Flat-topped or sugar-loaf cylindrical helmet; early century
- **Bascinet**: Pointed helmet fitting close to the skull; the newly developed **visored bascinet** was a key 14th-century innovation
- **Aventail**: Mail curtain hanging from the bascinet to protect the neck and throat
- **Cervelliere**: Simple skull cap worn under the great helm

**Torso:**
- **Hauberk**: Mail shirt reaching to mid-thigh or knee
- **Aketon / Gambeson**: Padded undergarment worn beneath mail
- **Jupon / Gipon**: Short fitted coat worn over armor, often displaying heraldic arms
- **Coat of Plates / Brigandine**: Transitional armor with plates riveted inside fabric

**Arms:**
- **Vambraces**: Forearm protection (plate or cuir bouilli)
- **Rerebraces**: Upper arm protection
- **Couters**: Elbow protection
- **Gauntlets**: Hand and wrist protection; evolved from mail mittens to articulated plate

**Legs:**
- **Chausses**: Mail leggings
- **Greaves**: Shin protection (plate)
- **Cuisses**: Thigh protection (plate or cuir bouilli)
- **Poleyns**: Knee protection
- **Sabatons**: Foot armor (plate); often pointed to match fashion

### Military Surcoat Fashion

A distinctive British fashion emerged around 1325-1340:
- The surcoat worn over armor was shortened to knee or mid-thigh length in front
- The back was left longer
- This style is valuable to scholars because it "neatly reveals the different layers of protection worn underneath"
- Despite fashion influence, armor modifications were limited where they would compromise "functional and protective qualities"

### Ordinary Soldiers

- Wore mail hauberks with boiled leather (cuir bouilli) knee and shin pieces
- Less plate armor than knights due to cost
- Simple helms (kettle hats or open-faced bascinets)
- Fabric gambesons sometimes served as primary armor for the poorest soldiers

---

## 7. Working Class Clothing

Documented in 14th-century *Tacuinum Sanitatis* (treatise on healthful living):

### Working Men
- Short or knee-length tunics
- Thick practical shoes
- For summer heat: simple shirts and braies only
- Hair typically uncovered
- Rough wool or undyed fabric

### Working Women
- Gowns with practical aprons
- Simple knotted kerchiefs for head covering
- Chemises for hot work
- Tucked-up gowns while performing manual labor
- Simpler, less decorated versions of upper-class garments

### Class Distinctions
- Servants received roughly one outfit per year as part of their remuneration
- Working people's garments were minimal and practical
- Nobility wore elaborate, expensive, multi-layered ensembles
- Sumptuary laws attempted to restrict certain materials and styles to specific social classes

---

## 8. Color & Pattern Reference

### Common Colors

| Color | Association |
|-------|------------|
| **Red** | Wealth and status; expensive dye |
| **Blue** | Common for wool; woad dye was widely available |
| **Green** | Common but prone to fading |
| **Yellow** | Affordable dye; sometimes associated with lower status |
| **Black** | Difficult to achieve in rich tones; became fashionable for nobility by late century |
| **White / Undyed** | Linen undergarments; poorest classes |
| **Purple** | Extreme luxury; reserved for highest nobility |

### Patterns & Decoration

- **Mi-parti**: Half-and-half contrasting colors
- **Checkered / Plaid**: Occasional appearance (e.g., Catalonian cotehardie combining reddish-brown and plaid)
- **Heraldic devices**: Arms displayed on surcoats and jupons
- **Animal roundels**: Italian silks with repeating animal motifs in circular frames
- **Embroidered motifs**: Animals, flowers, geometric patterns in gold and silver thread
- **Dagged edges**: Cut decorative borders on sleeves, hems, and hoods

---

## 9. Regional Variations

### England
- Elaborate court fashion under Richard II
- High collars, quantities of jewelry, patterned gold costumes with matching hats
- Strong heraldic tradition on military garments

### France
- Center of fashion innovation
- The 1393 *Bal des Ardents* in Paris featured men in elaborate fancy-dress with jeweled costumes
- Strong influence from the Burgundian court

### Italy
- Women permitted to show uncovered hair
- Lighter fabrics suited to warmer climate
- Leading silk production (Florence, Lucca, Venice)
- Byzantine and Eastern influence on textile patterns

### Hungary
- Fashion paralleled English styles by 1360
- Visible in the *Chronicon Pictum* (Illustrated Chronicle)
- Queen Barbara of Celje wore metal cauls around 1440

---

## 10. Key Visual Reference Points for Character Artists

When designing 14th-century characters for games, keep these visual markers in mind:

### Silhouette Indicators by Class

- **Nobility**: Long flowing garments, fur-lined, wide sleeves, pointed shoes, elaborate headwear
- **Knights**: Mix of mail and plate, heraldic surcoat, visored bascinet, sword and shield
- **Merchants**: Fine but less extravagant wool/silk, practical chaperon hat, belt with purse
- **Peasants**: Short tunic, simple shoes, minimal accessories, rough undyed fabric
- **Clergy**: Long robes, tonsured hair, simple colors (black, brown, white depending on order)

### Timeline Quick Reference

| Period | Key Fashion Change |
|--------|-------------------|
| 1300-1340 | Traditional long tunics; mail armor with early plate additions |
| 1340-1360 | Dramatic shortening of men's garments; tight-fitting doublets emerge |
| 1360-1380 | Arming coat (jupon) becomes fashionable outer wear; increasing plate armor |
| 1380-1400 | Houppelande appears; full plate suits develop; pointed shoes proliferate |
